review: Book 8: The book starts off with a bang and then just keeps it up throughout. After reading 7 books, there are some huge paybacks. Looking forward to the next and last book.

name: Leviathan Falls
author: James S. A. Corey
review: The last book in the series of 9 books. Incredible expectations. Lots of loose ends to tie up. Will it end with a satisfying exposition or will things be hurriedly resolved off-screen. Reading was exciting, the story kept pushing the pace so there never was a feeling that where's my ending. I could happily have read a couple more novels in continuation. And yes, the ending does satisfy things in a way that doesn't leave much to regret. Really solid writing, and what a job to deliver 9 books!

name: Shroud
author: Adrian Tchaikovsky
review: The best way I can describe this book is as if Jules Verne and Stephen Baxter had an adventure story written for Star Trek. Its simultaneously hard science fiction, as hard as it can get, with just a fantastically new and alien perspective on life and intelligence, and also a commentary of society and a truly grim outlook on the corporate capitalist nature of economy. The book itself is about aliens that evolved to treat the EM spectrum as communications medium without any sunlight, meaning only things that interact specifically with the EM bands are relevant. Human meatbags are not. Human made vehicles are. An adventure across the planet ensues. Brilliant, and extremely rewarding. I also somehow read this (hopefully) as a link to the next book in the Children of Time series. I'm going to be hunting for Tchaikovsky's books - the man has a talen for rapidly producing intelligent situations and writing multiple books in a year.

name: Dallergut Dream Department Store
author: Lee Min-ye
review: The premise of the book seemed alluring. A department store where you go buy dreams? An economy built on emotional reactions? A character plot promising hidden wisdom and a takedown of busy lifebodies? Sold! I bought the physical book. Turned out to be a dud. The premise seemed exceedingly made up and changing arbitrarily as if we are kids learning from story book time. There is no details given to characters beyond what seems like a single facet that you hold them to for the entire story. Feels too emotional reaction driven rather than being about actual ideas or stories. Disappointing.

name: How dare the sun rise
author: Sandra Uwiringiyimana, Abigail Pesta
review: This was part of the bookclub, so gave it a go. Was really into the story because its told so well, and its so human and real, and just made me more aware of different perspectives that I thought I knew but benefitted from seeing it from someone else's experiences. The story is about how the author escapes from a war torn country where they are effectively cuturally and socially uprooted, and their move to America with their parents, the adaption, and rising through sheer dedication and mindset. Admirable, and a good look on the world being more than a few ideas and people despite what we get exposed to.

name: Atlas of AI: Power, Politics, and the Planetary Costs of Artificial Intelligence
author: Kate Crawford
review: A recommendation for people looking into AI and its troubles. I think this was a bit too late for me, and in general felt aimed at someone else as I've had far too much exposure to these through my work. Recommended for people who want a more generalist perspective and would benefit from some additional details. The phrasing of 'atlas' really through me off, and I was expecting a more systematic structure and navigation. Instead the work means more about getting a perspective, which I've had from other books.

name: Valuable Humans in Transit and Other Stories
author: qntm
review: A collection of short stories, which deal on topics ranging from simulation to the nature of things. Quite entertaining, and reminiscient of Ted Chiang's foray, though these are more sci-fi focused.

name: Midnight Timetable
author: Bora Chung
review: A collection of ghost stories, I was initially patient that things might get more weirder, but the stories were all just meh. I understand there is an undercurrent in each that provides social commentary, but all of it felt so obviously placed, disconnected from the story itself, and also on the nose. So while interesting to read, none of the stories really stuck.

name: Living Things
author: Munir Hachemi
review: A short read about four (young) guys on a summer work program in Spain, and how their world is shaped through the experience. The writing is sharp and fluid, and keeps the story flowing. This would have been a good airport read. I didn't quite see the ending be done in a satisfying way, but that is also why the book is so short. Good writing, good storytelling.

name: Small Things Like These
author: Claire Keegan
review: An anti-Christmas carol, this is a story about a decent man finding a woman in distress in one of the Magdalene laundries. The book itself is small, and the writing flowing and episodic. It is a good book. Something to consider that the spirit of things is in our deeds more than the abstract notions of religion or celebration or any other thing. If you do not act, then you are not good.

name: Hamnet
review: A book about Shakespear. Quite unlike what I was expecting as this was a more gentle foray into a family, aware of prying but also desiring to share the state of things without focusing on the one man that became popular. I thought the story was quite sad, though entirely realistic and grounded, if that's even a sensible description. I picked up the book because this was the last book a dear friend was reading before their persistent absence, and I did not like that things had been left unfinished and unknown. I'm a little more at peace after finishing the book, which considering the story, I felt a strange emotional connection to as well. I know there is a move about the same, but I do not think I would want to watch it right away. Or ever. The book has been enough.

name: The AI Con: How to Fight Big Tech's Hype and Create the Future We Want
author: Emily M. Bender and Alex Hanna
review: An excellent book that explains, with the rigour of an academic and the patience of a public speaker, the background, working, and problems associated with the AI industry. Given that the authors are highly respected academics, there is indeed a plethora of informed opinions and references that build the case without overwhelming things. Definitely educational and timely.

name: Your Face Belongs to Us: A Tale of AI, a Secretive Startup, and the End of Privacy
author: Kashmir Hill

name: Feeding the Machine: The Hidden Human Labor Powering A.I.
author: James Muldoon, Mark Graham, Callum Cant
review: Decent book that could be more suitable for beginners or those who are not that. It does have stories about people though so that was interesting.

name: The New Age of Sexism: How the AI Revolution is Reinventing Misogyny
author: Laura Bates

name: The Age of Extraction: How Tech Platforms Conquered the Economy and Threaten Our Future Prosperity
author: Tim Wu

name: House of Day, House of Night
author: Olga Tokarczuk
review: Short stories by Olga Tokarczuk - yes please! Reminded me of flights in the most pleasant ways. But these stories are also about society, supersition, humour, and many other things. Delightful.

name: Fury
author: Salman Rushdie
review: Rushdie spins a tale of fury emerging from a man's psychie. Good writing, but not as sharp as other books. A bit whimsical, and having read his later works, almost feels like the start of an obsession with America.

name: Two Years Eight Months and Twenty-Eight Nights
author: Salman Rushdie

name: Mood Machine: The Rise of Spotify and the Costs of the Perfect Playlist
author: Liz Pelly
review: The book explains the story of Spotify, its capitalist background, and the impact it is having. While illuminating and educating (e.g. I didn't know the extent of spotify's ad origins) the book feels as if it has already selected the direction of what it wants to go towards and doesn't care if the reader wants to follow. Yes, I understand that companies like spotify have a huge amount of impact, and a lot of it may not be moral or good. So this is more a reflection on the structure and the writing.

name: AI Snake Oil: What Artificial Intelligence Can Do, What It Can't, and How to Tell the Difference
author: Arvind Narayanan, Sayash Kapoor
review: A well put together book by notable academics. Much of the book is the titular premise: that AI can be 'snake oil' and give false impressions or just not work despite the claims. The book seemed interesting for cases where one might not know the area, but otherwise it comes across as very surface level and not an actual foray into the topic itself. The later chapters about economic models felt incomplete by comparison. Still a good read for some of the references and to understand the style of explanation of when AI is not appropriate or does not work.

name: Lives of Bitter Rain
author: Adrian Tchaikovsky
review: A short novella set in the same world as the rest of tyrant philosphers. This is basically a mini version of things, with more events, more weird things, more tragedy, and a more tacit reminder of where our society is slipping.

name: One day all this will be yours
author: Adrian Tchaikovsky
review: I thought this novel would be the same style and setting as the previous one, which was absoluitely thrilling (walking to alderbaran). Imagine the surprise when the book starts off as a time traveller story, and a funny one at that. Totally unexpected from the author, which made the delight even more delightful. I would have expected this from Charles Stross maybe, but really hats off to stretching the imagination and fun here.

name: And put away the childish things
author: Adrian Tchaikovsky
review: Premise is simple: what if the child's fairy tale is not truncated and forgotten and the ill effects of when it goes on. Almost an anti-thesis of peter pan. Interesting, but didn't really pull the punches off the page like the rest of the books.

name: Saturation Point
author: Adrian Tchaikovsky
review: A short novella about eco-action. Nothing special to write. A lot of ideas seemed unsatisfactorily fleshed out. But the premise and the work itself was okay.

name: Pretenders to the Throne of God
author: Adrian Tchaikovsky

name: Enshittification: Why Everything Suddenly Got Worse and What to Do About It
author: Cory Doctorow
review: Doctorow coined the term enshittification. So it made sense that I had to read what he has to say about it. The book lays out the premise of a step-by-step intentional process with plenty of case studies. If nothing else, the book offers clear evidence of this being a market ploy and tactic. It is also clear about where things can be pointed to and what options could address it. Definitely will influence my work.

name: Bonsai
author: Alejandro Zambra
review: The patent blue Fitzcaraldo edition cover having caught my eye, I flicked the book open to see what new experiences might await me. The opening paragraph of this book hooked me immediately. A short book, I purchased it immediately. On a sunday morning, between coffee and breakfast, I finished all of it. It could have been 1000 pages to tell the same story, but the author reminded us with every few pages -- some things don't matter. Rarely can a talent cut across the essence of that story and deliver it like this. And that is what you are left with after -- just the essence. Not the body, which gives you a feeling of being full or having done a lot nor tiredness. This is the second book from the author and I think I will definitely want to serendipitously come across another some years in the future.

name: Babel, or the Necessity of Violence
author: R. F. Kuang
review: I am conflicted about how to think of this book. On the one hand there is no question that this is a clever book with the way that it draws allegories between language, magic, and colonialism. On the other hand, the story and the characters felt very much YA and superficial. The text was too dense in places, there was an avalanche of exposition. And there was no actual meaning to any of the story elements. I suspect this was very much a teenage story than an adult's foray into what colonialism does. While there is nothing wrong with it, and I would very much have enjoyed this book 20 years ago, I think it comes across as being much more sophisticated and for adults than warranted.

name: Close Encounters of the Fungal Kind
author: Richard Fortey

name: Looking for Jake and Other Stories
author: China Miéville
review: If there is one thing that is certain when reading China Mieville's stories, it is to expect the weirdness, the differentness, and sheer creativity. This is a collection of short stories that offer glimpses of ideas. The Tain was my favourite, but others felt like snippets of novels I have already read, which made me nostalgic. I also discovered that there is a new novel coming out later this year, so good timing I suppose.

name: Four-sided Triangle
author: William F. Temple
review: This was supposed to be old, as in really old WW era, science fiction. And while it was certainly slow, what came across was a story. Something that preceded many other ideas. A clone of your loved one who doesn't love you back, and what might that clone then do -- won't it love you? A simple premise that plays out as a social drama. Yes, there are elements of science fiction still in there, nearly half a century past. The sexism of the times does come across in places, sometimes strongly, such as when referring to asking permission from 'him' about 'her', but there is not a lot of it beyond the phrasing. I picked up a cheap copy - hardly 3 euros courtesy of the British Library. So this was a good exercercise in reading some older literature with a still interesting story.

name: Perspectives
author: Laurent Binet
review: A historical fiction that is clever in its presentation and alternate perspectives. Letters tell the story.

name: Lili is Crying
author: Hélène Bessette
review: A series of stories that tell the story of Lili, in a way that really shows the power of restraint in language, and the nuance of things in life.

name: Altered Carbon
author: Richard K. Morgan
review: I remembered enjoying the TV show quite a lot, but I always felt a lot of that was Joel Kinnaman's charm on the screen. Still, the book felt enticing, if only because the second season felt trash and the books promised redemption. Suffice to say that that book is excellent sci-fi and is enjoyable on its own. Went and purchased the next two books as well.

name: Inversions
author: Iain M. Banks
review: Culture novel but set in a world that doesn't even know the rest of the universe exists. And through this medieval lens, the author makes interesting arguments about socieity, intervention, morals, and most important of all - stories and what choices we make.

name: Titanium Noir
author: Nick Harkaway
review: Since the author always comes up with some bizzare twist on things, this felt enticing. It was, and the noir part does hold up to some extent. Overall a bit long, and the story doesn't get pulled as tightly as it could.

name: Love Machines: How Artificial Intelligence is Transforming Our Relationships
author: James Muldoon
review: Since I'm working in a project on AI companions, this was an essential read. The book does seem interesting but the end result is shallow. There are several aspects where it felt that there isn't enough exploration or nuance. End result: maybe the book helps someone who barely knows about the subject.

name: Mushrooms: A guide to fungi and toadstools
author: Liz O'Keefe

name: Creation Lake
author: Rachel Kushner
review: The reactions to this book are interesting. It seems that a lot of people picked up this book because it promised to be a 'spy thriller' (misleading marketing?) and it is to do with spying but to call it a thriller is definitely inaccurate. This is a book about society and individuality, masquedering or rather set in a spy setting. The premise is simple. But the writing and elaborations are the highlight. It demanded to be read slowly and carefully to absord the tinest of things, sometimes that came as fully formed, for example observations on the situation. The book also contains a large amount of fictitous correspondence with a crazy guy living in a cave who goes on an on about Neanderthals (as a rejection of who we are today?) Good book, interesting writing.

name: Empire of AI: Dreams and Nightmares in Sam Altman's OpenAI
author: Karen Hao
review: A seminal work from a reputed journalist, this book really explores the rise of OpenAI and tells a story that bares the reality of chaos. Essential reading for those working in this field.

name: The Political System of the European Union
author: Simon Hils

name: The Power of One: How I Found the Strength to Tell the Truth and Why I Blew the Whistle on Facebook
author: Frances Haugen
review: The infamous facebook whistleblower story. I picked up because the book showed some technical discussions from the author's own experiences. It was a good read, more for the amount of details put in and for the courage it took to come forward and expose things.

name: When the Moon Hits Your Eye
author: John Scalzi
review: A whacky story about what would happen if one day the moon suddenly turned to cheese. This is more of a commentary on the responses of the government, the internet bloggers, and other forms of poking fun at how we live absurd lives

name: Thirteen ways to kill Lulabelle Rock
author: Maud Woolf
review: A story about hiring your clone to kill other (consensual) clones because, what?, you got bored of them? So much promise in this one, and yet it felt forced, cliched, and just not satisfying. Also felt derivated, as in to the extreme.

name: Run
author: Blake Crouch
review: Things happen, they sound dangerous and globally threatening, so run. This was not as entertaining as other books, and in fact, felt very forced and artificial.

name: Starter Villain
author: John Scalzi
review: Inherit a villainish empire, have fun doing all the bond villain stuff. Entertaining, but nothing special otherwise.

name: The Census Taker
author: China Miéville
review: Deep story that demands thoughtfulness. But also difficult to read and understand the many many layers of thought behind each action, and the subtlety of shaping the story in a certain way. Are we sure we understood things correctly? Or do we just prefer to have a happy ending after all?

name: The Kaiju Preservation Society
author: John Scalzi
review: Not all books have to be deep complex stories and full of amazing world-building. Sometimes a book is like a good snack -- it is just enough to get you through a flight or a beach day. This is a good book for that sort of thing. There's that silly bit of pushing the plot along and then there;s some sprinkled bits about modernity - like mentioning the president since this book was written during covid. The overall pace moves along just well enough to not delve too deep nor bore you.

name: Nettle & Bone
author: T. Kingfisher
review: Fantasy but touched with reality. Knowing more about the hard choices one makes, especially as a woman, really shapes the story beyond just magic and action or whatever typical things are. Really nicely put together. Enough nuance to have things be conscious but not enough to feel in your face or that it isn't part of the story. The ending was a bit of a rush, but it still was enjoyable. In a weird way reminded me of reading Earthsea series.

name: Metronome
author: Tom Watson
review: A well written book with a simple premise, though I felt that some things were not as well developed. For example, without spoilers, there were plot devices that 'just worked' and had to work for the story to progress. That felt unrealistic. Apart from that this is a well written book and the two characters felt well thought out. Aside, this book should not be classified as sci-fi because there is a very little soft touch of actual science fiction here. It is still a good fiction book.

name: Piranesi
author: Susanna Clarke
review: The opening segment blasts you straight into a familiar feeling. Its a house with seemingly infinite rooms. There are variations, things of interest, and explorations. Hidden knowledge. Biomes. Wonders. What it reminded me of immediately was it someone had taken Borges' works and decided to really get into it. But there's more to this than just that. Towards the end, things collapse down into more predictable fantasies. But the writing is sharp, the nature of wonder and innocence and being human, that draws itself out in the confines of the House, is really the highlight. Can we see the wonders that pass by us everyday? Sometimes it takes another world to realise that.

name: The Vanished Birds
author: Simon Jimenez
review: The setting of the book is definitely within the bounds of scifi, what with the ability of a boy to teleport to any place he wants. But the core of the book is what it means to be a human in a society -- especially one that functions capitalistically. The boy is now a resource, for the benefit of mankind's progress of course, and the characters lives and decisions are all invisibly moved by the hand that takes things with an immeasurably invisible force and is reluctant to return any of it. There are colonies, corporations, subservient governments, and humans stuck in this way of life. The writing could have been better, the story does move at a very slow pace. But its undeniable what the book is.

name: Vagabonds
author: Hao Jingfang
review: The basic premise is that Mars has a colony that is a stand-in for China, and Earth for western ethos. Amidst this, we get some cultural exchange discussions which are supposed to discuss the egalitarian and moral values of both sides, their contrasts, and the necessities as justified by each, while also being biased to continue propogandeering their own side for continued existence. Intriguing in theory. But the book itself, which was quite long - twice as much, was filled with too much information, not an easy flowing story, and sometimes felt like a textbook with the characters being so flat and difficult to keep up with. Important events happen but we get no characterisations but the amount of information about cultural associations in even minor lessons is given several pages. This was a difficult read. Reminded me of Robinson and Le Guin in some of their works, which felt at least like they knew what they were after and you could feel the story take you there.

name: A River Called Time
author: Courttia Newland
review: This was a rather difficult book to read because it constantly felt like I was missing some context, some necessary background and understanding that would have made me realise the story and the events. The writing was not enjoyable and kept making me re-read things that I thought I had understood. The story itself seemed interesting, but the pacing and the characters felt underexplored. The themes, of capitalism, social discriminations, and revolution, were not particularly new nor novel.

name: Legends & Lattes
author: Travis Baldree
review: Comfort read in the style of DnD campaign shenanigans and cozy games where you level up a coffee shop (you've invented pastries! chocolate!) Set in a fantasy setting, this reminded me of the time I tried to introduce pizza in a remote setting and it was nice. The story itself is intentionally light and stress-free. Read it, relax, enjoy a cup of coffee.

name: The Will of the Many
author: James Islington
review: A large book, at 700 pages, but thoroughly entertaining. It felt like a weird mash-up of hunger games, harry potter, Chronicles, and many others. Still intriguing. The most interesting part was the way that Will works and the necessity of its flow to maintain governance. The setting of the Roman empire was also interesting. It straddles science and without spoiling the end, quite a lot, but the book is based on fantasy tropes. The most annoying one is how the protagonist seems to simply 'level up' and can do everything better than everyone else with little practice and ability.
